entities.name: Mirage
entities.raw_quote: a conjuration edited from two versions in the later folios is addressed to a spirit named Mirage, while an alternative form in the main block addresses Satan.

entities.name: Matthew the Spaniard
entities.raw_quote: 'Matthew the Spaniard' was utterly ignorant of this magic

entities.name: Socrates

attributes.role: supposed ancient authority cited by compiler

entities.raw_quote: Socrates himself, he claims, speaks of the power of one circle (no. 9) in his book of magic.

entities.name: Rawlinson necromantic manuscript
entities.raw_quote: The fifteenth-century writer of the Rawlinson necromantic manuscript tells how to summon a demon in the form of a horse by using conjurations, a ring with the Tetragrammaton inscribed on it, and a diagram made with the blood of a hoopoe or a bat.

entities.name: Tetragrammaton
entities.raw_quote: a ring with the Tetragrammaton inscribed on it

entities.name: hoopoe blood

attributes.use: in diagram for flying horse ritual

entities.raw_quote: a diagram made with the blood of a hoopoe or a bat

entities.name: bat blood
entities.raw_quote: a diagram made with the blood of a hoopoe or a bat

entities.name: red bridle horse
entities.raw_quote: a multitude of spirits will appear in the sky, with many horses; the magician must choose the horse with the red bridle

entities.name: Clm 849
entities.raw_quote: Twelve of the experiments in Clm 849 are ‘illusionist’ ones...

entities.name: De nigromancia / Thesaurus spirituum
entities.raw_quote: The ceremony given here for conjuring the horse is borrowed, perhaps directly, from the De nigromancia or Thesaurus spirituum ascribed to Roger Bacon.

entities.name: Sabbath feasts (demonic)
entities.raw_quote: the banquet that demons bring forth is in one particular like the feasts ascribed in later years to witches at their sabbaths: the food does not really exist...

entities.name: infernal hierarchy
entities.raw_quote: demons are bound by a perverse hierarchy of their own – an inversion of the angelic hierarchy

summary: This chunk discusses the Munich Handbook (Clm 849), focusing on its 'illusionist' necromantic experiments—those producing illusory spectacles like flying horses, banquets, castles, and invisibility. It highlights connections to other traditions (e.g., Arabic astral magic, Jewish divination, and later witch-trial concepts) and cites named spirits such as Mirage (with Satan as alternative), along with ritual tools like the Tetragrammaton ring and ingredients like hoopoe/bat blood. The Rawlinson manuscript’s flying horse rite is analyzed in detail and linked to the Thesaurus spirituum/De nigromancia tradition.
key_passages: a conjuration edited from two versions in the later folios is addressed to a spirit named Mirage, while an alternative form in the main block addresses Satan.
The ceremony given here for conjuring the horse is borrowed, perhaps directly, from the De nigromancia or Thesaurus spirituum ascribed to Roger Bacon.
Twelve of the experiments in Clm 849 are ‘illusionist’ ones, intended to make things appear other than as they are...
Socrates himself, he claims, speaks of the power of one circle (no. 9) in his book of magic.
the banquet that demons bring forth is in one particular like the feasts ascribed in later years to witches at their sabbaths: the food does not really exist...
